Why Is 3 < 2 < 1 True in JavaScript? Operator Precedence and Associativity
Day 16 of the reincarnation. This post is about operator precedence and associativity in JavaScript.
Every operator has a precedence you can look up. It's the same idea as math — when you do arithmetic, there's an order: multiply and divide before you add and subtract. JavaScript works the same way.
This matters because in real code you almost never have just one operator in an expression. You usually have several, so knowing which one runs first is pretty important.
So how do you know the precedence? I'll link the full docs later, but for now let's just cover a few common operators:

In your head you'd read this as: 1 is less than 2, and 2 is less than 3, so yeah, no problem. But what about this?
. And now you're probably confused — how is 3 less than 2, and how is 2 less than 1?
The thing is, this is an associativity issue. Like I said earlier,
when two operators have the same precedence, JavaScript falls back to associativity to decide what runs first
(3 isn't less than 2, after all), so the expression becomes this:

, and that's why the answer comes out

Once you've got this concept down, when you run into operators with the same precedence you'll instantly know which side runs first — you just might need to glance at the docs now and then.

Day 16 of the reincarnation. This post covers operator precedence and associativity in JavaScript. We'll look at why operators have a priority order, and the classic gotcha where 3 < 2 < 1 somehow evaluates to true. Once you understand associativity and type coercion, that weird result starts to make total sense.
